title: Introductions: Names, Origins, and First Questions
objectives: Introduce yourself naturally using me llamo and soy.
Ask someone their name formally and informally.
Say where you are from using soy de.
Choose between tu and usted in first-meeting situations.
Keep a simple introduction going with one follow-up question.

vocabulary.tip: Literally means I call myself. It is clear, polite, and perfect for first introductions.
Use soy for identity, name, origin, profession, and nationality.
Correct but more formal or official than me llamo.
Use with peers, children, friends, or relaxed situations.
Use with strangers, elders, clients, or anyone you want to address respectfully.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Good morning, what is your name?

vocabulary.tip: Use after answering a question to keep the exchange moving.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: My name is Tom. And you?

vocabulary.tip: Formal version of and you.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I am from Canada. And you?

vocabulary.tip: Use de to talk about where someone is from.
Use with people your age or in casual conversation.
Respectful version for strangers or formal introductions.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Excuse me, where are you from?

vocabulary.tip: Add a city, region, or country after de.
Use soy de Canada to say I am from Canada.
Mexico has the stress near the beginning: MEH-hee-ko.
The ñ sounds like ny in canyon.
Often shortened to los Estados Unidos.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I am from the United States.

vocabulary.tip: Use after names are exchanged.
Use encantado if the speaker is male and encantada if the speaker is female.
A natural response to mucho gusto or encantado.
Use to agree or add yourself to a statement.
Often optional because the verb already tells who is speaking.
Informal you. Do not confuse tu with tu, meaning your, when accents are shown.
Formal you. It uses third-person verb forms like es and se llama.
Use amiga for a female friend.
A respectful title for adult men.
A respectful title for adult women.

introductionPhrases.whenUsed: Use me llamo when you want to be clear and beginner-friendly. Use soy plus your name when the conversation is relaxed and natural.
Use with peers, friends, younger people, or relaxed first meetings.
Use with strangers, elders, service situations, or when you want to sound respectful.
Use after exchanging names in a casual conversation.
Use in respectful or professional introductions.
Use soy de plus a place to talk about origin.
Use after learning someone's name. Spain Spanish often uses encantado or encantada naturally.
Use as a polished response to mucho gusto or encantado.
Use informally after answering a name or origin question.
Use formally after answering a name or origin question.

grammar.explanation: Spanish introductions often use me llamo, soy, and de donde. The main choice is whether the conversation is informal with tu or formal with usted.

notes.title: Me llamo
notes.text: Me llamo means my name is, but literally it means I call myself. It is one of the safest first-introduction phrases.

notes.title: Soy
notes.text: Soy means I am. Use soy with your name, origin, nationality, and identity: Soy Tom. Soy de Canada.

notes.title: ¿Como te llamas?
notes.text: Use ¿como te llamas? when speaking informally. The te tells you the question is directed to tu.

notes.title: Formal vs informal you
notes.text: Use tu with peers and relaxed situations. Use usted with strangers, elders, clients, or anyone you want to treat respectfully.

notes.title: De donde / soy de
notes.text: Ask origin with ¿de donde eres? or ¿de donde es? Answer with soy de plus the place.

examples.spanish: Hola, me llamo Tom. ¿Como te llamas?
examples.english: Hi, my name is Tom. What is your name?
I am from Canada. And you?

grammar.practicePrompt: Introduce yourself, ask Carlos his name, and say where you are from.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Hello, my name is Tom.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Mucho gusto, Tom. Me llamo Carlos.
lines.english: Nice to meet you, Tom. My name is Carlos.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Hi, what is your name?

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.english: My name is Ana. And you?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Good morning, my name is Tom.

lines.speaker: Señora Ruiz
lines.english: Nice to meet you. What is your name?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Where are you from?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: I am from Canada.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: I am from Canada. And you?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: I am from Mexico.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Hi, I am Carlos.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: I am Tom. Nice to meet you.

lines.speaker: Lucia
lines.english: Hi, I am Lucia. Pleased to meet you.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Pleased to meet you, I am Tom.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Mucho gusto, señor. Me llamo Tom.
lines.english: Nice to meet you, sir. My name is Tom.

lines.speaker: Señor Lopez
lines.english: Nice to meet you, Tom.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Are you Carlos?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Yes, I am Carlos. And you?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Hola, me llamo Tom. Soy de Canada. ¿Como te llamas?
lines.english: Hi, my name is Tom. I am from Canada. What is your name?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Me llamo Carlos. Soy de Mexico. Mucho gusto.
lines.english: My name is Carlos. I am from Mexico. Nice to meet you.

pronunciation.focus: Name rhythm, double-l sound in llamo, and smooth question intonation.
tips: In llamo, ll often sounds like y in many Latin American accents.
In soy de, do not over-stress de; keep it short and light.
Questions often rise slightly at the end in beginner practice.
The ñ in España sounds like ny.

items.note: Let de connect softly into Canada.
The u in gusto is clear and strong.

listeningPhrases.english: Hi, my name is Tom. And you?

culture.title: Introductions are small, but they set the tone
culture.text: In Spanish-speaking settings, a complete introduction often feels warmer than only saying a name. A good first-meeting rhythm is greeting, name, nice to meet you, and one simple question. In Mexico, mucho gusto is widely useful and friendly. In Spain, encantado or encantada can sound very natural. When unsure, start with usted and let the other person invite a more informal tone.

nativeSpeech.title: How native speakers introduce themselves
notes: Native speakers often say soy plus their name in relaxed introductions.
Me llamo is still completely natural, especially when the exchange is clear and deliberate.
¿Como se llama? sounds more natural than always adding usted.
Mucho gusto can stand alone as a polished response.
People may answer origin with a city instead of a country: Soy de Guadalajara.

commonMistakes.mistake: Saying mi nombre es in every casual introduction.
commonMistakes.correction: Use me llamo or soy in everyday conversation.
commonMistakes.explanation: Mi nombre es is correct, but it can sound more formal or official.

commonMistakes.correction: Use ¿como se llama? in formal situations.
commonMistakes.explanation: The formal version is more respectful with strangers or elders.
Use de before the place you are from.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting to ask the other person's name.
commonMistakes.explanation: Introductions feel more natural when you invite the other person in.

commonMistakes.correction: Use usted with strangers, elders, and formal contacts.
commonMistakes.explanation: Usted creates a respectful tone until the relationship becomes more relaxed.

quiz.prompt: Choose the natural casual way to say I am Tom.
How do you ask what is your name informally?
How do you ask what is your name formally?
Choose the informal where are you from.
Which phrase is common in Spain for pleased to meet you?
Which phrase correctly says I am from Canada?

speakingChallenge.prompt: Introduce yourself casually and ask Carlos his name.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Hola, me llamo Tom. ¿Como te llamas?

speakingChallenge.prompt: Introduce yourself formally and ask someone's name.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Buenos dias, me llamo Tom. ¿Como se llama?

speakingChallenge.prompt: Say your name and where you are from.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Me llamo Tom. Soy de Canada.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Ask where Carlos is from and respond politely.
Create a full first-meeting exchange with greeting, name, origin, and a question.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Hola, soy Tom. Soy de Canada. ¿Como te llamas?

realLifeMission.title: Real Life Mission
realLifeMission.mission: Introduce yourself to Carlos and ask his name using at least three phrases from the lesson.
successCriteria: Use me llamo or soy with your name.
Ask Carlos his name with ¿como te llamas? or ¿como se llama?
Use at least one origin phrase, such as soy de Canada.
Include a polite phrase like mucho gusto or igualmente.
